definition: The estimated market value of a property after all renovations and repairs are completed. ARV is the foundation of every investment deal analysis — it tells you what the property will be worth when you sell or refinance. To calculate ARV, pull comparable sales (comps) of similar, recently renovated properties within a half-mile radius.

A legal transfer of a real estate purchase contract from the original buyer (wholesaler) to a new buyer (end investor). The wholesaler earns an assignment fee for connecting the seller with the buyer without ever taking ownership of the property. Assignment contracts must include an assignment clause in the original purchase agreement.

The profit a wholesaler earns when assigning a purchase contract to an end buyer. The fee is the difference between the wholesaler's contract price with the seller and the price the end buyer pays. Typical assignment fees range from $5,000 to $50,000 per deal depending on the property value and spread.

A real estate investing strategy that stands for Buy, Rehab, Rent, Refinance, Repeat. The investor buys a distressed property below market value, renovates it, rents it out, then does a cash-out refinance to recover the initial investment. The recovered capital is used to repeat the process on a new property, building a rental portfolio with recycled capital.

Capitalization rate — the ratio of a property's net operating income (NOI) to its purchase price, expressed as a percentage. Cap rate measures the expected annual return on an investment property independent of financing. Formula: Cap Rate = (NOI / Purchase Price) × 100. A higher cap rate indicates higher returns but typically higher risk. Most investors target 5-10% cap rates depending on the market.

The net income remaining after all expenses are paid on a rental property each month. Cash flow = Monthly Rent - Mortgage Payment - Property Taxes - Insurance - Maintenance - Vacancy Reserve - Property Management. Positive cash flow means the property generates profit each month. Most investors target $100-$300+ per unit per month in cash flow.

The annual cash flow divided by the total cash invested, expressed as a percentage. Unlike cap rate, cash-on-cash return accounts for financing — it measures the actual return on the money you put in, not the total property value. Formula: CoC = (Annual Cash Flow / Total Cash Invested) × 100. Most investors target 8-12% or higher.

Recent sales of similar properties in the same area used to estimate a property's market value. Good comps share similar characteristics: same neighborhood (within 0.5 miles), similar size (within 20%), similar age and condition, sold within the last 6 months. Comps are the foundation of property valuation for both investors and appraisers.

A report that compares a subject property to recently sold similar properties to estimate its market value. CMAs are prepared by real estate agents and investors using MLS data, public records, and comparable sales. A thorough CMA includes at least 3-5 comparable properties with adjustments for differences in size, condition, location, and features.

The ratio of a property's net operating income to its annual debt payments. DSCR = NOI / Annual Debt Service. A DSCR above 1.0 means the property generates enough income to cover its loan payments. Most lenders require a DSCR of 1.2-1.25 or higher for investment property loans. DSCR loans allow investors to qualify based on property income rather than personal income.

A wholesale exit strategy where two separate closings happen back-to-back — the wholesaler first buys the property from the seller (A-to-B closing), then immediately sells it to the end buyer (B-to-C closing). Unlike assignment, the wholesaler briefly takes title to the property. Double closings hide the wholesaler's profit from both parties and are useful when the assignment fee is large.

The investigation and research period after a property goes under contract but before closing. Due diligence includes property inspection, title search, reviewing liens and encumbrances, verifying property boundaries, checking zoning compliance, estimating repair costs, and confirming the property's value. Most purchase contracts include a due diligence period (typically 7-30 days) during which the buyer can back out.

A good-faith deposit made by the buyer to the seller when a purchase contract is executed, showing the buyer is serious about the transaction. EMD is typically held in escrow by a title company or attorney. For wholesale deals, EMD is usually $500-$5,000. For retail purchases, it is typically 1-3% of the purchase price. The deposit is credited toward the purchase at closing or refunded if the buyer backs out during the due diligence period.

The difference between a property's current market value and the amount owed on the mortgage. Equity = Market Value - Mortgage Balance. Equity builds through mortgage paydown (the tenant's rent pays off your loan), appreciation (property values increase over time), and forced appreciation (renovations that increase value). Investors can access equity through cash-out refinancing or selling.

A neutral third party (typically a title company or escrow agent) that holds funds and documents during a real estate transaction. Escrow ensures that the seller delivers clear title and the buyer delivers the purchase funds simultaneously at closing. The escrow agent also holds earnest money deposits during the contract period.

A real estate investment strategy where an investor buys a distressed property below market value, renovates it, and sells it for profit. The profit comes from the spread between the all-in cost (purchase price + rehab + holding costs + selling costs) and the sale price. Successful flippers buy at 65-75% of ARV minus repairs and target a minimum profit of $25,000-$50,000 per deal.

A quick metric for evaluating rental properties. GRM = Property Price / Annual Gross Rent. A lower GRM indicates a better deal. For example, a $200,000 property renting for $2,000/month has a GRM of 8.3 ($200,000 / $24,000). Most investors look for GRMs under 10 for cash-flowing properties. GRM is a rough screening tool — always follow up with a full cash flow analysis.

A short-term, asset-based loan provided by private lenders (not banks) for real estate investments. Hard money loans are based on the property's value (typically 65-75% LTV) rather than the borrower's creditworthiness. Terms: 6-18 month duration, 10-14% interest, 2-4 origination points. Hard money is ideal for fix-and-flip projects and bridge financing where speed matters more than cost.

An investment strategy where you buy a small multifamily property (duplex, triplex, or fourplex), live in one unit, and rent out the others. The rental income from the other units covers most or all of your mortgage payment, effectively letting you live for free while building equity. House hacking qualifies for owner-occupied financing (FHA loans with 3.5% down), making it one of the lowest capital entry points into real estate investing.

The ratio of a loan amount to the appraised value of the property, expressed as a percentage. LTV = Loan Amount / Property Value × 100. A $150,000 loan on a $200,000 property has a 75% LTV. Lower LTV means less risk for the lender and typically better loan terms for the borrower. Most conventional loans require 75-80% LTV (20-25% down payment) for investment properties.

The highest price an investor should pay for a property based on the deal analysis. The standard formula for wholesale/flip deals: MAO = ARV × 70% - Repair Costs - Assignment Fee (if wholesaling). This ensures there is enough profit margin for the end buyer while leaving room for the wholesaler's fee. Some investors use 65% or 75% depending on market conditions and risk tolerance.

A property owner who has a strong reason to sell quickly, often willing to accept a below-market price in exchange for speed and certainty. Common motivations include pre-foreclosure, inherited property, divorce, job relocation, tax delinquency, costly repairs, tired landlord syndrome, and code violations. Finding motivated sellers is the core skill in wholesaling and discounted acquisitions.

The annual income a property generates after all operating expenses but before debt service (mortgage payments). NOI = Gross Rental Income - Operating Expenses. Operating expenses include property taxes, insurance, maintenance, property management, vacancy allowance, and utilities (if owner-paid). NOI does NOT include mortgage payments, depreciation, or capital expenditures. NOI is the basis for calculating cap rate.

An individual (not an institutional lender) who loans their own money for real estate investments. Private money lenders are typically friends, family, colleagues, or members of real estate investment groups. Terms are negotiable and usually more flexible than hard money — interest rates of 6-12%, longer terms, and less rigid qualification requirements. Building relationships with private lenders is a key scaling strategy for investors.

The total cost of renovating an investment property, including materials, labor, permits, and contractor fees. Accurate rehab estimation is critical — underestimating rehab costs is the #1 reason flips lose money. Common rehab categories: kitchen ($15-30K), bathrooms ($8-15K each), flooring ($3-8K), paint ($3-5K), roof ($5-15K), HVAC ($4-10K), foundation ($5-20K). Always add a 10-20% contingency buffer.

The percentage return on the total money invested in a real estate deal. ROI = (Net Profit / Total Investment) × 100. For flips: Net Profit = Sale Price - Purchase Price - Rehab - Holding Costs - Selling Costs. For rentals: use annual cash flow or total return (cash flow + appreciation + equity paydown + tax benefits). ROI should be compared to alternative investments to determine if the deal is worth pursuing.

A detailed document listing every repair, renovation, and improvement to be made on an investment property. The SOW includes specific tasks (demo, framing, electrical, plumbing, etc.), materials specifications, labor estimates, and a timeline. A clear SOW is essential for getting accurate contractor bids, managing the renovation budget, and avoiding scope creep that inflates costs.

The process of finding contact information (phone number, email, mailing address) for a property owner using public records, databases, and data aggregation services. Skip tracing is essential for off-market deal finding — once you identify a potentially motivated seller's property, you need their contact info to make an offer. Popular skip tracing services include BatchSkipTracing, TLOxp, and REISkip.

A creative financing strategy where the buyer takes ownership of a property 'subject to' the existing mortgage remaining in the seller's name. The buyer makes the mortgage payments but the loan stays in the seller's name. This allows investors to acquire properties without qualifying for a new loan. Risks include the due-on-sale clause (lender could call the loan due) and the seller remaining liable for the mortgage.

A tax-deferral strategy under IRS Section 1031 that allows investors to sell an investment property and reinvest the proceeds into a 'like-kind' property without paying capital gains taxes on the sale. Requirements: the replacement property must be identified within 45 days and closed within 180 days. A qualified intermediary must hold the funds. 1031 exchanges allow investors to scale their portfolio without losing capital to taxes.

A quick formula used by wholesalers and flippers to determine the maximum purchase price for an investment property. The rule states: Maximum Offer = ARV × 70% - Repair Costs. The 30% margin covers closing costs, holding costs, selling costs, and profit. Example: a property with an ARV of $200,000 and $30,000 in repairs should be purchased for no more than $110,000 ($200,000 × 0.70 - $30,000).

The rate at which available homes are sold in a specific market during a given time period. Absorption Rate = Number of Homes Sold / Total Available Inventory. A high absorption rate (above 20%) indicates a seller's market with high demand. A low rate (below 15%) indicates a buyer's market with excess supply. Investors use absorption rate to gauge market health and determine pricing strategy.

The increase in a property's value over time. There are two types: natural appreciation (driven by market forces like population growth and inflation, typically 3-5% annually) and forced appreciation (created by the investor through renovations, better management, or rezoning). Appreciation is one of the four wealth-building pillars of real estate alongside cash flow, principal paydown, and tax benefits.

A professional assessment of a property's market value conducted by a licensed appraiser. Lenders require appraisals before issuing mortgages to ensure the property is worth at least the loan amount. Appraisers use comparable sales, property condition, location, and market trends to determine value. For investors, the appraisal is critical for refinancing — it determines how much equity you can access.

Small directional signs (typically 18×24 inches) placed at intersections and high-traffic areas with messages like 'We Buy Houses' or 'Cash for Your Home.' Bandit signs are a low-cost marketing method used by wholesalers and investors to generate motivated seller leads. They cost $1-5 each and can generate 5-15 calls per sign per week. Note: many cities have ordinances against bandit signs, so check local laws before placing them.

A person who finds potential real estate deals and passes them to investors in exchange for a referral fee (typically $500-$2,000 per deal). Bird dogging is an entry-level role in real estate investing — it requires no capital or contracts, just the ability to identify distressed or undervalued properties. Bird dogs are essentially lead generators for wholesalers and investors.

Tax owed on the profit from selling an investment property. Short-term capital gains (properties held less than 1 year) are taxed as ordinary income (up to 37%). Long-term capital gains (properties held more than 1 year) are taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income. Investors can defer capital gains using 1031 exchanges or offset them with depreciation.

An investor who purchases properties with cash rather than financing. Cash buyers can close faster (7-14 days vs 30-45 days with financing), have no appraisal contingencies, and can often negotiate better prices due to the certainty of closing. Wholesalers sell primarily to cash buyers. Building a cash buyers list is essential for successful wholesaling.

A refinancing strategy where you replace your existing mortgage with a new, larger loan and pocket the difference in cash. For example, if your property is worth $200,000 and you owe $120,000, you could refinance at 75% LTV ($150,000) and receive $30,000 in cash. This is the 'Refinance' step in the BRRRR method — it allows you to recover your initial investment and redeploy it into the next deal.

Fees and expenses paid at the closing of a real estate transaction beyond the purchase price. Buyer closing costs typically include title insurance, escrow fees, recording fees, loan origination fees, and prepaid taxes/insurance (usually 2-5% of purchase price). Seller closing costs include real estate agent commissions (5-6%), transfer taxes, title fees, and outstanding liens. Investors must factor closing costs into their deal analysis.

Non-traditional methods of funding real estate deals that go beyond conventional bank loans. Creative financing strategies include seller financing, subject-to, lease options, private money, hard money, partnerships, self-directed IRAs, and wraparound mortgages. These strategies allow investors to acquire properties with less money down, no bank qualification, or more favorable terms than traditional financing.

The number of days a property has been listed for sale on the MLS before going under contract. DOM is a key market health indicator — low DOM (under 30 days) indicates a hot seller's market, while high DOM (over 90 days) indicates a slower buyer's market. Properties with high DOM may indicate overpricing or property issues, and sellers with high-DOM listings are often more motivated to negotiate.

A tax deduction that allows real estate investors to deduct the cost of the building (not land) over its useful life — 27.5 years for residential and 39 years for commercial properties. Depreciation reduces taxable income without requiring any actual cash expense. For example, a $275,000 building can be depreciated at $10,000 per year, reducing your tax bill even while the property appreciates in value.

A marketing strategy where investors send physical letters, postcards, or yellow letters to targeted property owners to generate motivated seller leads. Effective direct mail campaigns target specific lists: absentee owners, pre-foreclosures, tax delinquent properties, probate, and high-equity homeowners. Response rates typically range from 0.5-3% with an average cost of $0.50-$2.00 per piece.

A property in poor condition or facing financial distress such as foreclosure, tax delinquency, or code violations. Distressed properties are typically sold below market value because the owner cannot afford repairs or mortgage payments. These properties represent the primary deal source for wholesalers, flippers, and BRRRR investors. Signs of distress include overgrown lawns, boarded windows, vacant appearance, and accumulated mail.

A deal-finding strategy where investors drive through target neighborhoods looking for distressed or vacant properties. When a potential deal is spotted, the investor records the address, then uses skip tracing to find the owner's contact information and reach out with an offer. Driving for dollars is one of the cheapest and most effective lead generation methods — it costs only gas money and can be enhanced with apps like DealMachine.

A mortgage insured by the Federal Housing Administration that requires only 3.5% down payment for owner-occupied properties. FHA loans are popular for house hacking — buying a 2-4 unit property, living in one unit, and renting the others. FHA loans have lower credit requirements (580+ credit score) and lower down payments than conventional investment property loans, making them an accessible entry point for new investors.

The process of attracting and identifying potential motivated sellers or investment property leads. Lead generation methods include direct mail, cold calling, text messaging, PPC advertising, driving for dollars, bandit signs, networking, and AI-powered property search tools like AIWholesail. The quality and volume of leads directly determines deal flow and revenue for real estate investors.

The period between when a homeowner receives a notice of default from their lender and the actual foreclosure auction. During pre-foreclosure, the homeowner can still sell the property to avoid foreclosure — often at a discount because of urgency. Pre-foreclosure lists are available through county records and data services. These homeowners are among the most motivated sellers because they face losing their property entirely.

A financing arrangement where the property seller acts as the lender instead of a bank. The buyer makes monthly payments directly to the seller according to agreed-upon terms (interest rate, duration, down payment). Seller financing eliminates bank qualification requirements, appraisals, and lengthy approval processes. Common terms: 5-10% down, 6-9% interest, 5-30 year amortization with a 3-7 year balloon payment.

A company that verifies legal ownership of a property, conducts title searches, issues title insurance, and facilitates the closing process. Title companies ensure the seller has clear title (no outstanding liens, judgments, or ownership disputes) before the buyer takes ownership. In wholesale transactions, the title company holds the earnest money, processes the assignment, and distributes funds at closing.

An examination of public records to verify the legal ownership of a property and identify any liens, encumbrances, easements, or claims against it. A clear title means there are no outstanding issues that could prevent or complicate the sale. Title searches reveal mortgage liens, tax liens, mechanic's liens, judgments, and ownership history. Never close on a property without a clear title search.

Short-term financing (typically 1-3 days) used by wholesalers for double closings. A transactional funder provides the cash needed to purchase the property from the seller (A-to-B closing) and is repaid when the wholesaler immediately resells to the end buyer (B-to-C closing). Transactional funding fees are typically 1-2% of the purchase price and eliminate the need for the wholesaler to use their own capital.

The percentage of time a rental property sits empty without a paying tenant. Vacancy rate is a critical factor in cash flow analysis — most investors budget 5-10% for vacancy when projecting income. Formula: Vacancy Rate = (Vacant Days / Total Days) × 100. Market-wide vacancy rates indicate rental demand — rates below 5% suggest strong demand, while rates above 10% indicate oversupply.

A real estate investment strategy where an investor (wholesaler) puts a property under contract at a discounted price and then assigns that contract to an end buyer for a fee, without ever purchasing or owning the property. Wholesaling requires minimal capital, no renovation experience, and no property ownership. It is the most common entry point for new real estate investors and generates income through assignment fees.

A form of seller financing where the seller carries a new mortgage that wraps around their existing mortgage. The buyer makes one payment to the seller, who then continues making payments on the original loan. The seller profits from the interest rate spread between the two loans. Wraps are used in creative financing deals but carry risk if the buyer defaults or the underlying lender discovers the arrangement.

Local government regulations that dictate how a property can be used — residential, commercial, industrial, mixed-use, or agricultural. Zoning affects what you can build, how many units a property can have, and whether you can operate a business from the location. Investors should always verify zoning before purchasing — rezoning can dramatically increase a property's value, while zoning restrictions can limit your investment strategy.
